0.text: The thirty-fourth year passed away, and also the thirty-fifth, and the disciples of Jesus had established a church of Christ in all the surrounding lands. Everyone who came to them and truly repented of their sins was baptized in the name of Jesus, and they also received the Holy Ghost.

In the thirty-sixth year, all the people on the entire face of the land were converted to the Lord, both Nephites and Lamanites. There were no arguments or disagreements among them, and everyone treated each other fairly.
They shared everything they had, so there were no rich or poor, no enslaved or free, but they were all equal and shared in the heavenly gift.
The thirty-seventh year also passed away, and there was still peace in the land.

The disciples of Jesus performed great and marvelous works. They healed the sick, raised the dead, made the lame walk, gave sight to the blind, and hearing to the deaf. They performed all kinds of miracles among the people, and they did none of these things except in the name of Jesus.

And so the thirty-eighth year passed away, and also the thirty-ninth, and the forty-first, and the forty-second, and on through to the forty-ninth year, and the fifty-first, and the fifty-second, and even to the fifty-ninth year.
The Lord blessed them greatly in the land, so much that they rebuilt cities where cities had been burned.
They even rebuilt the great city of Zarahemla.
But many cities had sunk into the ground and water had come up in their place, so those cities could not be rebuilt.
The people of Nephi grew strong and multiplied very quickly, and became a very beautiful and pleasant people.
They married and were given in marriage, and were blessed according to the many promises the Lord had made to them.

They no longer followed the rituals and ordinances of the law of Moses, but instead followed the commandments they had received from their Lord and God, continuing in fasting and prayer, and meeting together often to pray and hear the word of the Lord.
There was no conflict among any of the people in all the land, and mighty miracles were performed among the disciples of Jesus.

The seventy-first year passed away, and the seventy-second, and so on until the seventy-ninth year. A full hundred years had passed, and the disciples of Jesus whom he had chosen had all gone to the paradise of God, except for the three who were to remain on the earth. Other disciples were ordained in their place, and many of that generation had also passed away.
There was no conflict in the land because of the love of God that filled the hearts of the people.

There was no jealousy, no fighting, no unrest, no immorality, no lying, no murder, and no wickedness of any kind. Truly, there could not have been a happier people among all the people God had ever created.
There were no robbers or murderers. There were no longer Lamanites or any other group divisions. They were united as one people, the children of Christ and heirs to the kingdom of God.

How blessed they were! The Lord blessed them in everything they did. They were blessed and prospered until a hundred and ten years had passed away, and the first generation after Christ had passed away, and there was no conflict in all the land.
Nephi, who kept this last record (and he kept it on the plates of Nephi), died, and his son Amos kept the record in his place, also on the plates of Nephi.

Amos kept the record for eighty-four years, and there was still peace in the land, except for a small group of people who had left the church and taken upon themselves the name of Lamanites. So there began to be Lamanites again in the land.

Amos also died (it was now one hundred and ninety-four years since the coming of Christ), and his son Amos kept the record in his place. He also kept it on the plates of Nephi, and it was also written in the book of Nephi, which is this book.
Two hundred years had now passed away, and the second generation had all passed away except for a few.

Now I, Mormon, want you to know that the people had multiplied so much that they were spread across the entire face of the land, and they had become extremely rich because of their prosperity in Christ.
In this two hundred and first year, some began to be lifted up in pride, wearing expensive clothing and all kinds of fine pearls and costly things of the world.
From that time on, they no longer shared their possessions with each other.
They began to divide into social classes, and they began to build up churches for profit, and began to deny the true church of Christ.

By the time two hundred and ten years had passed, there were many churches in the land. Many of them claimed to know Christ, yet they denied most of his gospel. They accepted all kinds of wickedness and gave sacred things to people who were unworthy to receive them.
This false church grew rapidly because of wickedness and because of the power of Satan, who took hold of their hearts.

There was also another church that denied Christ entirely. They persecuted the true church of Christ because of their humility and belief in Christ, and they looked down on them because of the many miracles that were performed among them.

So they used their power and authority against the disciples of Jesus who remained with them. They threw them into prison, but by the power of the word of God that was in them, the prisons were split apart, and they went out performing mighty miracles among the people.
Despite all these miracles, the people hardened their hearts and tried to kill them, just as the Jews in Jerusalem had tried to kill Jesus, as he had predicted.
They threw them into furnaces of fire, and they came out unharmed.
They also threw them into dens of wild animals, and they played with the wild animals like a child plays with a lamb. They came out from among them without any harm.

Still, the people hardened their hearts because they were led by many priests and false prophets to build up many churches and do all kinds of wickedness. They attacked the people of Jesus, but the people of Jesus did not fight back. And so the people continued to decline in unbelief and wickedness year after year, until two hundred and thirty years had passed away.
In this year, the two hundred and thirty-first year, there was a great division among the people.
In this year, a people arose who were called Nephites, and they were true believers in Christ. Among them were those the Lamanites called Jacobites, Josephites, and Zoramites.
So the true believers and worshipers of Christ (including the three disciples of Jesus who were to remain) were called Nephites, Jacobites, Josephites, and Zoramites.

Those who rejected the gospel were called Lamanites, Lemuelites, and Ishmaelites. They did not simply drift away from belief but deliberately rebelled against the gospel of Christ. They taught their children not to believe, just as their ancestors had done from the beginning.

This was because of the wickedness and wrongdoing of their ancestors, just as it had been from the beginning. They were taught to hate the children of God, just as the Lamanites had been taught to hate the children of Nephi from the start.
By the time two hundred and forty-four years had passed away, this was the state of the people. The more wicked part of the people grew strong and became far more numerous than the people of God.
They continued to build up churches for themselves and decorate them with all kinds of precious things. And so two hundred and fifty years passed away, and also two hundred and sixty years.
The wicked part of the people began once again to establish the secret oaths and conspiracies of Gadianton.
Even the people called the people of Nephi began to be proud in their hearts because of their great riches, and became vain like their brethren, the Lamanites.
From this time on, the disciples began to grieve over the sins of the world.
When three hundred years had passed, both the Nephites and the Lamanites had become extremely wicked, just like each other.
The robbers of Gadianton spread across the entire land. No one was righteous except the disciples of Jesus. People hoarded gold and silver in great amounts and traded in all kinds of commerce.
After three hundred and five years had passed (and the people still remained in wickedness), Amos died, and his brother Ammaron kept the record in his place.

When three hundred and twenty years had passed, Ammaron, prompted by the Holy Ghost, hid the sacred records — all the sacred records that had been passed down from generation to generation — until the three hundred and twentieth year from the coming of Christ.
He hid them for the Lord's purposes, so they could come again to the remnant of the house of Jacob, according to the prophecies and promises of the Lord. And so ends the record of Ammaron.
